title: Dedicated tenant batching-tier override not taking effect (route policy appears to win over tenant override)
preview:
Issue summary
- Dedicated customer reports that their tenant-level override for batching SLO tier (fast) is not being applied; effective behavior remains standard.
- Customer expectation (and internal design): customer override > route policy > tier default.
- Observed: route policy appears to override tenant setting (or tenant setting is ignored).

Customer impact
- Customer is attempting to force fast tier on a latency-sensitive streaming endpoint.
- They are seeing increased queueing and higher p99 compared to pre-tiered-defaults behavior.
- Impact limited to a subset of routes on their Dedicated cluster, but blocks their rollout of tiered defaults.

Environment
- Dedicated cluster: northlake-dedicated-03
- Region: us-east
- Runtime train: 1.22.0 (feature flag enabled for tiered defaults)
- Control plane: 2026.04.08 build (policy evaluation service)

What the customer configured
1) Tenant override (Dedicated Console > Cluster > Runtime overrides): batching_tier=fast
2) Route policy (Console > Routes > /v1/chat/completions): batching_tier=standard (set earlier during testing)

Expected behavior
- Requests on that tenant/cluster should run fast tier regardless of route policy.

Actual behavior
- Requests continue to use standard tier.
- Telemetry shows selection_reason=route_policy for the affected route.

Steps to reproduce (internal)
1) In staging (or a dedicated test cluster), set tenant override batching_tier=fast at the cluster/tenant scope.
2) Set route policy batching_tier=standard on a specific route.
3) Send requests without request-level tier headers.
4) Observe effective tier via decision trace / metrics label.

Observed signals
- runtime.metric: redwood_inference_request_latency_ms{tier="standard",route="/v1/chat/completions"} increases.
- reason code emitted: profile_selection_reason="route_policy" even when tenant override is set.

Notes
- Customer also noted that toggling the tenant override off/on sometimes changes behavior after ~10 minutes, suggesting config propagation/cache involvement.

Requested outcome
- Fix precedence to match published behavior for Dedicated.
- Provide an immediate workaround + ETA for rollout to their cluster.

title: SEV-2: eu-west latency spike after runtime build 2026.03.18.0 (kernel selection)
preview:
Issue summary: p95 latency regression for long-context traffic in eu-west after runtime build 2026.03.18.0.

Impact: SLO burn for chat long-context bucket; customers saw slower responses but low error rate.

Environment: prod eu-west, A100 pools.

Investigation notes: Suspected kernel selection heuristic change; perf-canary shows regression aligned with build.

Mitigation: Disable heuristic via feature flag; force kernel to prior stable variant.

title: Suspicious mass auth attempts after internal "job application" form; possible credential harvest impacting service accounts
preview:
Summary: On 2026-03-13 09:42 UTC the security monitoring alerting pipeline flagged a clustered spike of failed and then some successful token exchanges from several service accounts following a widely-circulated internal "job application" Google Form. The pattern suggests credentials may have been harvested via the form and subsequently used to attempt programmatic access.

Impact: Multiple service-account API tokens and a small number of CI systems observed anomalous auth activity. No confirmed data exfiltration detected so far. Some internal CI jobs failed due to token rotation triggered after suspicious logins. Customer-facing services are not known to be affected.

Detection: Real-time SIEM rules (auth-anomaly-001) and the anomaly detector for token-exchange rate detected a burst between 09:25–09:47 UTC with (>120) failed refreshes and 7 successful refreshes originating from 3 public IPs not previously seen in the org. Alert fired to #security and #oncall.

Initial hypothesis: Public Google Form (internal-only link accidentally made public) was used to collect resumes; attackers embedded a credential-harvesting redirect or 3rd-party widget that captured employee credentials. Harvested credentials then used to authenticate service principals (shared secrets stored in CI) or to exchange for short-lived tokens via our IdP.

Immediate containment actions taken:
- Rotated affected service-account keys and CI secrets for flagged projects (automated rotations executed 09:55–10:20 UTC).
- Blocked originating IPs at the cloud firewall and added them to blocklist in WAF (completed 10:05 UTC).
- Disabled the public job form and removed external sharing; notified GSuite admin to revoke any third-party app consents.
- Temporarily enforced stricter token issuance policy for our IdP for 30m window (reduced refresh window).

Next steps (short term):
- Complete audit of all service accounts that had token activity during the window (Owner: IAM team).
- Run credential exposure checks against recent form submissions (Owner: Security Ops).
- Identify whether any of the 7 successful token exchanges correspond to privilege escalation actions in Cloud Audit Logs.
- Revoke any suspicious sessions and ensure CI pipelines use rotated secrets.

Longer-term follow-ups:
- Review internal form sharing policies and update onboarding docs to require security review for externally embedded widgets.
- Add a SIEM rule to detect sudden token-exchange success rate following a high volume of failed refreshes.
- Postmortem and employee notification plan if any account compromise confirmed.

Contact: Security Ops (pager), Oncall SRE, Identity team.

title: Centralize ephemeral labeling-seat pool for LabelWave pilot (SCIM + BYO-KMS) and align renewal to FYQ3
preview:
Issue summary:
We need to onboard vendor 'LabelWave' for a 6-week labeling pilot. The vendor requires: SCIM provisioning for ephemeral contractor accounts, BYO-KMS support for storing exported label artifacts, and a single invoice aligned to our FY Q3 renewal window.

Impact:
- Research & Data team cannot start the pilot until seat provisioning is available.
- Contractors currently require manual invites which increases onboarding time by ~2 days per contractor and creates security auditing gaps.
- If renewal dates are not aligned, we will incur multiple pro-rated invoices and extra procurement overhead.

Request / Acceptance criteria:
1) Create a centralized ephemeral license pool (50 seats) that can be handed out to contractors and reclaimed automatically via SCIM deprovisioning.
2) Configure SCIM integration with LabelWave and map attributes: email, manager, cost_center tag.
3) Validate BYO-KMS workflow (AWS KMS key ARN provided by Redwood) — ensure LabelWave can encrypt exported artifacts using our key or confirm an approved alternative.
4) Finance: route invoices to Cost Center: RDT-40 and consolidate renewal to align with Redwood FY Q3 start date (2026-07-01).
5) Security: run a short intake checklist (SaaS security questionnaire + SOC2 evidence) and provide a go/no-go.
6) Deliver a short runbook for on-call and CS to manage seat reclamation and emergency access.

Attachments (uploaded to ticket): contract_draft_LabelWave_2026-03-08.pdf; vendor_security_answers_LabelWave_2026-03-09.xlsx

title: Restricted forensic sample extract for privacy-incident reproduction
preview:
Issue summary:
A customer reported a potential PII leak in generated outputs from an application using redwood/longform-v1. We need a narrowly scoped forensic sample extract from production inference logs to reproduce the issue and validate whether PII appeared in model outputs.

Justification:
- Reproduction required to determine root cause (model prompt leakage vs. prompt content).
- Small, time-boxed sample minimizes exposure.

Scope of data requested:
- Time window: 2026-03-11 00:00:00 to 2026-03-11 23:59:59 (UTC)
- Up to 200 inference records matching the customer tenant and the reported conversation hashes.
- Include fields: request_id, customer_tenant_id (hashed), request_prompt (redacted), response_text (original and redacted copy), model_version, routing_info, timestamp, and system metadata (instance id, region).
- Explicit exclusion: do NOT include raw user identifiers or customer-provided file blobs.

Redaction & security controls proposed:
- Hash customer_tenant_id with HMAC-SHA256 and store salt in KMS-scoped key; original id not stored in extract.
- Run automated PII DLP scanner on response_text and mask email addresses, phone numbers, and long (>=16) numeric sequences. Keep an unmasked copy in an encrypted S3 object encrypted with customer-specific KMS key accessible only to approvers for a maximum of 7 days.
- Write-only S3 path with ACL restricted to the approved list; enable S3 object lock and server-side encryption with KMS.
- Access audit: all downloads require an IAM session with MFA and are logged to the security audit trail.

Retention & disposal:
- Extract TTL: 7 calendar days from extraction completion; automated deletion triggered by lifecycle rule.
- Any derived artifacts (screenshots, local copies) must be ephemeral and deleted after use; users must attest to deletion in this ticket.

Approval needed from:
- Security (data handling and key access)
- Legal/Privacy (customer notice & contract review)
- Engineering (SRE or runtime owner to run job)

Impact if delayed:
- Slows customer response and may escalate to incident; potential regulatory exposure if PII is confirmed.

Requested owner and run plan:
- Owner: Ethan Park (SRE) to run extraction job with help from Infra tooling.
- Run plan: run a narrow query against tensorflow-prod-logs table, filter by customer tenant hash, limit 200 rows, pipe to DLP scanner, package results to secure S3 with metadata manifest and HMAC verification.

Acceptance criteria:
- Security and Legal sign-off documented in comments.
- Extract contains only scoped fields and passes DLP masking policy.
- TTL and audit controls in place and verified.

title: Provisioning/Repair: Fleet power policy rollout locks CPU at low P-state causing Windows container start failures
preview:
Issue summary:
A recent Intune-driven corporate power policy rollout applied to a subset of developer and infra workstations appears to be forcing minimum processor states and other AC power parameters that lock CPU performance at the lowest P-state on some systems. Affected machines show sustained ~400 MHz CPU frequency on AC power after provisioning/re-imaging which causes Windows containers (Docker Desktop and Windows Server containers used for local dev) to fail health checks and refuse to start reliably.

Impact:
- Several internal engineers and platform developers cannot start local containers; CI jobs that rely on Windows container hosts fail on self-hosted runners on affected machines.
- Issue observed on ~12 confirmed devices in the us-west fleet; likely more unreported.
- Productivity impact (blocked local dev, intermittent CI failures). No customer-facing outage.

Environment:
- OS: Windows 11 Enterprise 22H2 (corporate image)

title: Temporary telemetry query privilege for data-minimization audit
preview:
Issue summary:
Request from Compliance (Privacy) to run a constrained set of telemetry queries against production request logs to confirm that new data-minimization rules have reduced retention of user identifiers in prefix caches. This is not an export request; Compliance needs aggregated metrics and a small number of sampled rows to validate masking rules.

Background:
- New data-minimization policy shipped 2026-03-05 adjusted KV cache retention and redaction on session keys.
- Compliance requires a short verification window to confirm no PII leakage in the last 7 days of production telemetry.

Impact / Risk:
- High sensitivity: production logs contain request metadata and potentially user-provided content.
- Goal is to avoid broad access to raw payloads and ensure least-privilege.

Requested access:
- JIT (just-in-time) query privilege for Compliance engineer: Duration 4 hours, single session, scoped to aggregated queries + up to 10 sampled rows with deterministic redaction applied.
- Queries will run through the observability query-console behind bastion with session recording and enforced masking UDFs.

Justification and minimality plan:
- Compliance will only need: counts, distinct user_id counts, percentage of requests containing raw email patterns, and up to 10 sampled requests where identifiers are replaced with stable tokens (tokenization) and free-text redaction applied.
- No full payload dumps, no key-value cache export. If any sample row contains unexpected PII after redaction, session is terminated and incident escalated.

Access controls and safeguards requested:
- Role granted: telemetry_read_jit (timebound IAM role that maps to a short-lived certificate).
- Network: access only via secure bastion (AUDIT-LOGS flagged), from Compliance laptop (registered device).
- Query pre-approval: Compliance to provide queries in advance. Only pre-approved queries allowed; ad-hoc queries blocked by policy.
- Data transformations: apply built-in redaction UDFs to sampled rows and mask user identifiers in the result set.
- Audit: all queries and results captured in the audit trail; copies retained for 90 days in encrypted bucket.

Duration and rollback:
- Access window: 2026-03-16 09:00 to 2026-03-16 13:00 US/Eastern (4 hours).
- If any policy violation or unexpected PII is observed, access revoked immediately and IR lead notified.

Acceptance criteria / Success criteria:
- Compliance obtains aggregated metrics showing reduction in identifier retention according to the new policy across the last 7 days.
- Sampled rows (max 10) show tokenized identifiers and redacted free text.
- No raw payloads or unredacted identifiers are exported.

Attachments (queries to run):
1) COUNT(*) by day for last 7 days with prefix-cache-hits filter.
2) DISTINCT(user_id) count and histogram of session length.
3) Regex-based scan for email patterns; return counts and top-5 matching request IDs (masked).
4) Return up to 10 sampled rows after redaction UDF applied (sampling seeded for reproducibility).

Next steps requested from SRE/Security:
- Create timebound IAM role and JIT certificate for the compliance principal.
- Pre-register the bastion session with recording enabled and notify IR lead.
- Validate redaction UDFs with a dry-run on staging using synthetic data prior to production run.

title: Windows 11 roaming + adapter power-save causing WireGuard route ghosting and DNS failures on corporate Wi‑Fi
preview:
Issue summary:
Windows 11 laptops intermittently lose connectivity to internal services after roaming between APs. The WireGuard interface remains up but the system installs a stale 0.0.0.0/0 route pointing at the local wifi gateway (metric low), effectively blackholing outbound traffic for processes bound to the wg interface. DNS queries either fail or go to the wrong resolver.

Impact:
- ~8 active users in the SF office reporting repeated failures during heavy roaming (moving between floors / boardroom).
- Symptoms: applications time out (internal dashboards, git pushes), developer shells lose connections, Slack remains connected but messages to internal services fail.

Environment:
- OS: Windows 11 22H2 (build 22631.x)
- Wireless chipset: Intel AX211 / AX201 (driver 22.50.1 reported on affected machines)
- VPN: WireGuard v0.7.6 (enterprise config via enterprise-agent)
- Zero-trust/agent: redwood-edge v2.1.0 (manages wg interface and DNS overrides)
- SSID: Redwood-Corp (802.11ax, controller-managed fast-roaming/FT enabled)

Steps to reproduce:
1) Connect to Redwood-Corp from a Windows 11 laptop with WireGuard active.
2) Walk between two nearby APs or trigger a roam using the vendor utility (force roam).
3) Observe that user retains WPA2/WPA3 session but some processes lose access to internal services.
4) Run 'wg show', 'route print', and 'nslookup internal-service'.

Observed behavior / logs (excerpt):
- route print shows 0.0.0.0 -> 192.168.10.1 metric 10 (preferred) coexisting with wg0 route 0.0.0.0 -> 10.10.0.1 metric 50; processes bound to wg0 still attempt outbound but packets never egress correctly.
- WireGuard peers show keepalives delayed; 'wg show' shows handshake timestamps stall for 30–120s.
- Windows System event: WLAN AutoConfig reported 'Roaming completed' then 'Driver queued packets during power save transition'.
- tcpdump on edge gateway shows missing keepalives during DTIM handover windows.

Initial hypothesis:
- Wireless driver power-management / DTIM handling during fast roaming causes packet queuing and delayed keepalives; WireGuard's endpoint detection thinks tunnel still healthy but routing table gets re-ordered when interface metrics are recalculated.
- Controller fast-roaming timers (FT) combined with Windows power profile and driver implementation create a race where the default route is re-inserted before wg interface routes stabilize.

Impact assessment:
- P1 due to multiple developers blocked during peak hours; intermittent but reproducible in SF office.

Investigation notes:
- Reproduced on 2 lab machines with same Intel AX211 driver.
- Disabling 'Allow the computer to turn off this device to save power' on the WLAN adapter immediately eliminated the symptom on test devices.
- Changing Windows Power Plan (Wireless Adapter Settings -> Power Saving Mode = Maximum Performance) also mitigated.
- On the controller side, reducing FT roam aggressiveness and increasing reassociation hold time reduced the frequency but did not fully eliminate.
- Packet captures show keepalives delayed by up to 90s during roam; WireGuard handshake resumes but application sockets experience default TCP timeouts.
- Intune profile was used to push temporary registry settings and wireless power plan to a pilot group (10 machines) with success.

Mitigation applied:
- Deployed Intune device configuration to pilot cohort (10 users in SF) to set Wireless Adapter power to Maximum Performance and to disable the 'allow device to sleep' option.
- Rolled controller config change to throttle FT handoffs to be less aggressive (increase reassociation timer to 500ms).
- Sent bug report to Intel with packet captures and driver logs; vendor acknowledged potential driver-side race and requested additional telemetry.

Next steps / plan:
- Expand Intune mitigation to all SF office laptops (scheduled phased rollout over 3 days).
- Validate Intel driver fix when available; test in lab (matrix: Windows builds x driver versions x WG config).
- Add a customer-side guard in redwood-edge agent: detect roaming handshake gap > 10s and force reassertion of wg route metrics (work queued as GH PR).
- Update wireless runbook with mitigation steps and add monitoring rule for 'wg handshake gap' on fleet.

Related artifacts and links:
- Packet captures: s3://redwood-captures/INT-864209/roam-pcap-0309.pcap (internal)
- Controller config change PR: https://git.internal.redwood.net/network/configs/pull/122

Impact:

- Symptoms: applications time out (internal dashboards, git pushes), developer shells lose connections, Slack remains connected but messages to internal services fail.

Environment:
- OS: Windows 11 22H2 (build 22631.x)
- Wireless chipset: Intel AX211 / AX201 (driver 22.50.1 reported on affected machines)
- VPN: WireGuard v0.7.6 (enterprise config via enterprise-agent)
- Zero-trust/agent: redwood-edge v2.1.0 (manages wg interface and DNS overrides)
- SSID: Redwood-Corp (802.11ax, controller-managed fast-roaming/FT enabled)

Steps to reproduce:
1) Connect to Redwood-Corp from a Windows 11 laptop with WireGuard active.
2) Walk between two nearby APs or trigger a roam using the vendor utility (force roam).
3) Observe that user retains WPA2/WPA3 session but some processes lose access to internal services.
4) Run 'wg show', 'route print', and 'nslookup internal-service'.

Observed behavior / logs (excerpt):
- route print shows 0.0.0.0 -> 192.168.10.1 metric 10 (preferred) coexisting with wg0 route 0.0.0.0 -> 10.10.0.1 metric 50; processes bound to wg0 still attempt outbound but packets never egress correctly.
- WireGuard peers show keepalives delayed; 'wg show' shows handshake timestamps stall for 30–120s.
- Windows System event: WLAN AutoConfig reported 'Roaming completed' then 'Driver queued packets during power save transition'.
- tcpdump on edge gateway shows missing keepalives during DTIM handover windows.

Initial hypothesis:
- Wireless driver power-management / DTIM handling during fast roaming causes packet queuing and delayed keepalives; WireGuard's endpoint detection thinks tunnel still healthy but routing table gets re-ordered when interface metrics are recalculated.
- Controller fast-roaming timers (FT) combined with Windows power profile and driver implementation create a race where the default route is re-inserted before wg interface routes stabilize.

Impact assessment:
- P1 due to multiple developers blocked during peak hours; intermittent but reproducible in SF office.

Investigation notes:
- Reproduced on 2 lab machines with same Intel AX211 driver.
- Disabling 'Allow the computer to turn off this device to save power' on the WLAN adapter immediately eliminated the symptom on test devices.
- Changing Windows Power Plan (Wireless Adapter Settings -> Power Saving Mode = Maximum Performance) also mitigated.
- On the controller side, reducing FT roam aggressiveness and increasing reassociation hold time reduced the frequency but did not fully eliminate.
- Packet captures show keepalives delayed by up to 90s during roam; WireGuard handshake resumes but application sockets experience default TCP timeouts.
- Intune profile was used to push temporary registry settings and wireless power plan to a pilot group (10 machines) with success.

Mitigation applied:
- Deployed Intune device configuration to pilot cohort (10 users in SF) to set Wireless Adapter power to Maximum Performance and to disable the 'allow device to sleep' option.
- Rolled controller config change to throttle FT handoffs to be less aggressive (increase reassociation timer to 500ms).
- Sent bug report to Intel with packet captures and driver logs; vendor acknowledged potential driver-side race and requested additional telemetry.

Next steps / plan:
- Expand Intune mitigation to all SF office laptops (scheduled phased rollout over 3 days).
- Validate Intel driver fix when available; test in lab (matrix: Windows builds x driver versions x WG config).
- Add a customer-side guard in redwood-edge agent: detect roaming handshake gap > 10s and force reassertion of wg route metrics (work queued as GH PR).
- Update wireless runbook with mitigation steps and add monitoring rule for 'wg handshake gap' on fleet.

Related artifacts and links:
- Packet captures: s3://redwood-captures/INT-864209/roam-pcap-0309.pcap (internal)
- Controller config change PR: https://git.internal.redwood.net/network/configs/pull/122

title: Staggered autoscale window: transient GPU attrition + noisy-tenant correlated throughput collapse
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports large p99/p50 throughput collapse during the daily autoscale window (09:00-09:12 UTC) for their dedicated pool. Impact: production request latency p99 jumped from ~700ms to 6–12s, and sustained token throughput dropped by ~55% for ~8 minutes.

Impact:
- Customer-facing inference frontends experienced long tail latencies and 5xx spikes (mostly 502/504).
- Several requests timed out causing downstream job failures in ScribeAI pipelines.
- Business impact: queued jobs and delayed processing for ~350k inference tokens.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool id: dp-78a4 (ScribeAI reserved cluster)
- Region: us-east-1
- Serving runtime version: v2.18.3
- Autoscaler version: v1.9.1
- Model variants: redwood/gpt-4o-mini-v1 pinned at 2026-03-10

Steps to reproduce (customer provided):
1) Send steady QPS ~25 with mixed short/long streams between 08:50-09:10 UTC.
2) Wait for autoscale window at 09:00 UTC (predictive increase expected).
3) Observe sudden p99 and throughput collapse between 09:02–09:10 and intermittent 502s.

Observed behavior:
- Autoscaler detached 2 GPU nodes in staggered fashion (09:01:30 and 09:02:10) while queue depth briefly rose.
- Several GPU attachments failed with NVLink handshake WARNs in node logs and one driver reset observed (dmesg recorded nvidia-mig reset).
- A noisy tenant in the same reserved pool produced many short-lived high-frequency multiplexed streams at the same time (traffic spike on tenant-id t-1946).

Expected behavior:
- Autoscaler should avoid removing capacity mid-window when queue depth is above the hot threshold and should not induce driver resets.

Logs/metrics provided:
- link to customer dashboard (redacted): https://console.redwood.ai/dp-78a4/metrics?from=2026-03-12T08:30&to=2026-03-12T09:30
- kernel/driver excerpt attached by customer (snippet included in comments).

title: Concurrent client retries split transaction boundary causing duplicate tool executions across Python and Go SDKs
preview: Issue summary: Customer reports duplicated external side effects (webhook POST to order processing) when their app issues concurrent retries from both Python (2.4.1) and Go (1.7.0) SDK clients in a short window. Impact: Duplicate orders reaching downstream systems, billing exposure, and customer trust concern. Observed behavior: Under burst failure conditions the orchestrator appears to split what the client treats as a single transactional interaction into two distinct server-side request groups, and two tool invocations are delivered to the downstream webhook. Expected behavior: A single external tool call (webhook) per logical user action even if the client retries locally; SDK/platform should provide clear idempotency guarantee guidance and mechanisms to prevent duplicate side-effects.

title: TCP timestamp stripping by corporate firewall causes intermittent gRPC reconnects and degraded streaming in private deployment
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers on a private VPC deployment intermittently observe gRPC stream resets and frequent reconnects when sending long-lived streaming inference requests. Impact: streaming clients experience token duplication, reconnect churn, and increased tail latency, causing transient user-facing failures during payments reconciliation jobs.

Impact:
- Multiple customers reported reconnect storms during peak batch inference windows (FinNova observed ~8% request failures, clients retried and duplicated outputs).
- Tail latency increases from 120ms median to >1.5s p95 for streaming responses.

Environment:
- Deployment: Private (customer-managed VPC peering + egress through corporate firewall)
- Region: us-east-1 (customer-routed egress)
- Redwood runtime: serving-runtime v2026.02.10
- OBS: TLS stays up; application-level gRPC shows RST and transient EOFs.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Customer runs a long-lived streaming generation job (streaming chat with >1024 token context) against a pinned redwood-7b-v2 model on their private deployment.
2) Load generator runs 100 parallel streams per node for ~30 minutes.
3) Observe intermittent request resets and reconnects in client logs; server sees TCP-level retransmits and occasional out-of-order segments.

Observed behavior:
- gRPC status CANCELLED / UNAVAILABLE on clients with reconnects multiple times per minute.
- Kernel/tcpdump on serving node shows TCP timestamp fields stripped or zeroed on some packets once flow exits through corporate firewall.
- When timestamps are stripped, TCP duplicate-detection heuristics mis-evaluate sequence numbers under reordering, leading to spurious retransmits and head-of-line blocking.

Expected behavior:
- No mid-path modification of TCP options (timestamps) for established flows; stable long-lived gRPC streams with graceful keep-alives.

Logs/Traces:
- Attached extract (sanitized) from serving node tcpdump: repeated TCP options length mismatch and TSval=0 in packets after 2026-03-12T15:21:10Z.
- Stackdriver/Datadog spans: multiple grpc:stream closed events correlated with increased retransmit counters on node NIC.

Temporary workaround:
- Customer routed egress around corporate firewall for a subset of serving nodes (temporary egress NAT through a managed firewall) and saw immediate reduction in reconnects.
- Short-term mitigation: reduce concurrent streams per node and enable smaller keepalive intervals to force faster recovery while we investigate.

Next steps:
- Coordinate with customer networking to capture full pcap across egress path and corporate firewall appliance.
- Reproduce in controlled staging with a middlebox that strips TCP timestamps.
- Evaluate runtime-level mitigation (disabling reliance on RFC1323 TS for duplicate detection in our user-space TCP stack or tuning kernel parameters) and a longer-term recommendation for corporate firewall config (preserve TCP options).

title: Intermittent 5xx spike from prefetch-circuit coupling causing memory pressure and runtime restart loop
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer (ChorusAI) reported a sudden elevation in 5xx responses on streaming and short-request routes starting 2026-03-13 14:10 UTC. Errors manifest as 502/503 from API gateway, and upstream runtimes in the customers dedicated pool repeatedly restart. Impact: customer reports degraded UX (failed chat completions) and elevated token-level retries.

Impact:
- ~35% error rate on the affected route for the tenant during the peak window.
- Multiple upstream runtime processes observed OOM/restarts.
- Customer requests are predominantly small/prefix-heavy (streaming chat) mixed with a small embedding upload job.

Environment:
- Dedicated tenant cluster in us-east-1, production.
- Autoscaler: dedicated-gpu-pool-v3.

Steps to reproduce (observed in production):
1. Tenant performs a short burst of streaming chat traffic (100-200 RPS) concurrently with a 50k-row embedding upload pipeline.
2. API gateway begins shedding/hedging requests; some requests placed in prefetch queue.
3. Prefetch circuit engages (queue backpressure + cancellation), then a wave of downstream runtime OOMs and restarts occurs, followed by API gateway 502/503 responses.

Observed at: 2026-03-13 14:10 UTC onward.

title: Unexpected 5xx surge from accept-queue / prefetch resonance causing runtime preemptions
preview:
Issue summary:
A sustained elevation in 5xx responses originating at the API gateway was observed between 2026-03-12 09:05 UTC and ongoing. Root cause appears tied to an interaction between accept-queue growth and aggressive prefetch / KV warmup on dedicated runtimes, which amplifies upstream latency and triggers GPU preemption (watchdog) on some ephemeral workers.

Impact:
- Customer: VertexDocs (dedicated) reported ~35% failed requests for chat and embedding routes during the window.
- User-facing: increased 5xx and 503/502 responses, elevated tail latency on chat-generation endpoints, degraded throughput for concurrent embedding batches.
- Business: Affected production traffic across a single us-east shard; no billing anomalies reported.

Environment:
- Deployment: Dedicated pool (us-east) serving VertexDocs tenant (dedicated).
- Runtime: inference-runtime v2.14.1 with kernel selection v1.8.
- API Gateway: apigw v3.2.5 behind edge LB.

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern):
1) Sudden concurrent mix of small chat requests (n ~200/s) + large embedding batch uploads from tenant.
2) APIGW accept-queue depth grows > 512 for ~30s; prefetch subsystem begins parallel warmup and KV writes.
3) Serving runtime threads see long blocking latencies (io/warmup) -> backlog -> GPU watchdog preemption on ephemeral workers.
4) Upstream exits / SIGTERM -> APIGW returns 502/503 and hedged retries amplify the load.

Observed behavior / logs: 
- APIGW metrics: accept_queue_depth spike to 640, 5xx rate jumped from baseline 0.2% to 18% at 09:06 UTC.
- Runtime logs (ephemeral worker): repeated lines: "GPU watchdog: preempting process (stall detected)" followed by "SIGTERM received - draining" and exit code 137 on a subset of workers.
- Prefetch controller: concurrent_kv_writes = 48 (above recommended 12 for dedicated pool), lock contention traces on kv-shard-17.
- Kernel traces: increased pagefaults and GPU memory fragmentation warnings preceding preempt.

Expected behavior:
- Accept queue should remain within configured thresholds; prefetch concurrency should be throttled before upstream worker preemption occurs. No production-grade runtimes should be preempted by short-lived warmup spikes.

Investigation notes (work in progress):
- Hypothesis A: Prefetch concurrency amplification under mixed small-chat + large-embed workloads causes blocking IO on worker threads, increasing service time and accept-queue growth.
- Hypothesis B: Hedged retry logic at APIGW amplifies already-backlogged requests causing a feedback loop; retries dropped onto the same shard provoke a stampede.
- Observed correlation: workers that hit preemption had higher KV write rates and heavier NVMe iowait.
- Cross-checks: No recent model swap or hotpatch during window. Autoscaler did spawn new ephemeral workers but they entered preempt loops quickly.

Mitigations applied so far:
- Rolled out an immediate temporary APIGW rule to cap hedged retries for the VertexDocs tenant (reduces amplification).
- Reduced prefetch concurrency for the affected dedicated pool from 48 to 8 (config hotpatch) to reduce KV write contention.
- Manually restarted a subset of ephemeral workers after clearing NVMe caches and increasing threadpool IO budget.

Next steps / action items:
- SRE: create a short-term circuit that rejects prefetchs above 16 concurrent per-shard (owner: Ethan Park) - due 2026-03-14.
- Runtime team: add telemetry to correlate kv-write latencies and GPU watchdog triggers (owner: Priya Rao) - due 2026-03-20.
- API gateway: evaluate hedged retry heuristics to avoid retry amplification on accept-queue backpressure (owner: API Platform) - due 2026-03-18.
- Postmortem: capture exact sequence and propose permanent fix (locking/queuing strategy) once full logs are available.

Resolution: Investigating - temporary mitigations in place, traffic restored to ~98% successful for the tenant after mitigations.

Relevant links:
- Grafana: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/acceptqueue/accept-queue-overview?var=shard=us-east
- Logs (sample): https://logs.redwood.ai/q=SUP-6123456-sample

title: Steady 429s for mixed file-upload + function-calling streams after burst-window config change
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer LumenDocs is seeing sustained 429 responses for a subset of requests after we applied a change to their dedicated pool burst window (reduced from 10s to 4s). The requests are a mix of file uploads (multipart POST -> upstream file store) followed immediately by function-calling streams that open a streaming websocket for model inference.

Impact:
- ~12% of user sessions experience immediate 429s when the client uploads a file and triggers a function-calling stream within 2s.
- Customer reports degraded UX for their document ingestion + extract pipeline.
- Affects one dedicated pool (LumenDocs-prod-us-west).

Environment:
- Redwood Dedicated pool: lumen-prod-pool-7
- Region: us-west (single AZ)
- Client SDK: JS v2.5.1 (multiplexed websocket + fallback to http), custom upload proxy in front of SDK
- Quota config: per-tenant token bucket + short burst window (4s) after config change on 2026-03-11 03:20 UTC

Steps to reproduce (provided by customer):
1) Start 50 concurrent sessions that each: upload a ~3–4MB PDF via multipart POST; after upload completes, immediately call function-calling API which opens a streaming websocket and sends the initial prompt to the model.
2) Observe that ~10–15% of the streams receive a 429 at connect time (error returned by API gateway).
3) If the function-calling call is delayed by >6s after upload, 429 rate drops to baseline (~0.5%).

Attachments / Logs provided:
- Short HAR capture from the client showing pattern (attached as har_lumendocs_20260312.zip).
- API gateway logs: gateway-logs-2026-03-12-10.gz (shows 429 codes with rate_limiter.reason=burst_window_exceeded).
- Service metrics: dedicated-pool-lumen-prod-7-2026-03-11-03:00-04:00.png

Investigation notes:
- Initial hypothesis: reducing the burst window compressed token accounting from 10s to 4s and caused overlapping uploads + stream opens to be charged against the same short window, exhausting per-tenant concurrency tokens.
- Confirmed in rate-limiter traces: high simultaneous token consumption events ~1.2k tokens/s at time of peak; many requests were multipart upload HEADERS and the subsequent websocket connect counted as new tokens immediately after file upload completion.
- Gateway reports reason=burst_window_exceeded for 429 responses. Traces show token bucket refills happening on 4s cadence after config change.
- Not a serving-runtime OOM/noisy neighbor symptom: GPU metrics for the pool remained within targets and there are no 5xx spikes in model servers.

Temporary workaround suggested to customer:
- Stagger the client to add a 6s delay between upload completion and function-calling stream start. This drops the 429 rate to baseline.
- Revert their dedicated pool burst window to 10s as a config change (SRE/ops action), which their team is willing to try if we confirm safety.

Next steps / Action items:
- (Assigned) Nadia to validate rate-limiter accounting logic and confirm whether upload finalization + websocket connect are double-counted in the same bucket interval.
- (Assigned) Nadia to run a replay test in staging with short burst window (4s) using LumenDocs traffic pattern reproducer.
- (Support) Recommend a short-term pool config rollback for customer if replay reproduces issue.
- (Product) Evaluate whether gateway should depress counting for multipart file upload handshake (100-continue / finalization) to avoid immediate double charge when followed by a streaming connect.

Resolution notes (pending):
- Customer accepted staggered client delay as a temporary mitigation while we validate config.
- If confirmed, schedule a follow-up change to rate-limiter to improve accounting and then restore shorter burst window if still desired for other workloads.

Steps to reproduce (provided by customer):
1) Start 50 concurrent sessions that each: upload a ~3–4MB PDF via multipart POST; after upload completes, immediately call function-calling API which opens a streaming websocket and sends the initial prompt to the model.
2) Observe that ~10–15% of the streams receive a 429 at connect time (error returned by API gateway).
3) If the function-calling call is delayed by >6s after upload, 429 rate drops to baseline (~0.5%).

Attachments / Logs provided:
- Short HAR capture from the client showing pattern (attached as har_lumendocs_20260312.zip).
- API gateway logs: gateway-logs-2026-03-12-10.gz (shows 429 codes with rate_limiter.reason=burst_window_exceeded).
- Service metrics: dedicated-pool-lumen-prod-7-2026-03-11-03:00-04:00.png

Investigation notes:
- Initial hypothesis: reducing the burst window compressed token accounting from 10s to 4s and caused overlapping uploads + stream opens to be charged against the same short window, exhausting per-tenant concurrency tokens.
- Confirmed in rate-limiter traces: high simultaneous token consumption events ~1.2k tokens/s at time of peak; many requests were multipart upload HEADERS and the subsequent websocket connect counted as new tokens immediately after file upload completion.
- Gateway reports reason=burst_window_exceeded for 429 responses. Traces show token bucket refills happening on 4s cadence after config change.
- Not a serving-runtime OOM/noisy neighbor symptom: GPU metrics for the pool remained within targets and there are no 5xx spikes in model servers.

Temporary workaround suggested to customer:
- Stagger the client to add a 6s delay between upload completion and function-calling stream start. This drops the 429 rate to baseline.
- Revert their dedicated pool burst window to 10s as a config change (SRE/ops action), which their team is willing to try if we confirm safety.

Next steps / Action items:
- (Assigned) Nadia to validate rate-limiter accounting logic and confirm whether upload finalization + websocket connect are double-counted in the same bucket interval.
- (Assigned) Nadia to run a replay test in staging with short burst window (4s) using LumenDocs traffic pattern reproducer.
- (Support) Recommend a short-term pool config rollback for customer if replay reproduces issue.
- (Product) Evaluate whether gateway should depress counting for multipart file upload handshake (100-continue / finalization) to avoid immediate double charge when followed by a streaming connect.

Resolution notes (pending):
- Customer accepted staggered client delay as a temporary mitigation while we validate config.
- If confirmed, schedule a follow-up change to rate-limiter to improve accounting and then restore shorter burst window if still desired for other workloads.

title: Burst 502/503s from GPU worker thread starvation during large-embedding job with keepalive churn
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers observe short bursts (~30s-2m) of 502/503 responses originating at the API gateway when a large embedding job (approx 1.2M tokens across many small requests) runs concurrently with normal traffic. Rate of errors correlates with embedding job spikes and periods of many short-lived streaming sessions (keepalive churn).

Impact:
- LuminaSearch (dedicated) reported 15-30% request error-rate increase during bursts, impacting live search indexing.
- Errors are transient but repeatable with similar workload pattern; downstream retries amplify load.

Environment:
- Prod, us-east, Dedicated GPU pool (A100s), serving-runtime v2026.02.7, nvml driver v470.x, API gateway v1.14.3

Steps to reproduce (approx):
1) Start large embedding job: 12k concurrent small embedding requests batched by customer SDK into many parallel connections.
2) Introduce a stream of short-lived streaming sessions to simulate keepalive churn (many short connections).
3) Observe API gateway 502/503 spikes and increased watchdog restarts on runtime.

Observed logs / metrics:
- api-gateway logs show upstream reset / 502 with code "upstream connection closed" during bursts.
- serving-runtime logs contain repeated lines: "watchdog: gpu-worker-thread timeout, resetting worker pid=***" and occasional "nvml timeout waiting for response (2000ms)".
- GPU utilization shows high but not fully saturated. CPU on runtime host shows high runnable thread count in process responsible for driver IO.
- KV-cache eviction rate spikes, prefetch queue length increases.

Notes on customer session:
- Customer reported increased retrying logic in their client; they attempted backoff which reduced but did not eliminate the bursts.
- No config changes on customer side during incident window.

title: Microburst pipelining causing GPU starvation and autoscaler stall in dedicated pool
preview: Issue summary: ClearView reported a sharp degradation in p50/p99 latency and a throughput collapse for routes pinned to their dedicated pool. The behavior coincided with a burst of many short-lived streaming inference requests from the customer's application. Impact: multiple ClearView routes experienced 5-10x p99 latency increases for ~20 minutes during business hours; customer-facing degradation triggered a support SLA escalation. Timeline: 2026-03-12 09:02 UTC — first alert from customer; 09:05 — support triaged and opened ticket; 09:20 — SRE observed sustained GPU queue depth and slot utilization anomalies; 09:45 — applied temporary mitigation (reserve slots), 10:10 — mitigation restored baseline for ClearView. Observed behavior: - High slot fragmentation: dozens of slots occupied briefly by short streaming sessions, preventing efficient batching. - Autoscaler did not scale up despite high queue depth because burst duration fell below smoothing thresholds. - Serving runtime recorded frequent enqueue/dequeue stalls and occasional KV cache thrashing. - GPUs reported low per-GPU utilization but many pending microbatches queued, suggesting scheduling lock contention rather than raw capacity shortage. Hypothesis: A combination of many short streaming connections (sub-second request durations) and the autoscaler's sampling/decision window caused the controller to miss the microburst signal. The serving scheduler's pipeline/slot allocator prioritized fairness, leading to many partially-filled slots and serialization that prevented efficient kernel batching. Immediate mitigation: - Enabled emergency slot reservation for ClearView (force min_alloc_slots=4) which reduced p99 to baseline within ~25 minutes. - Activated a temporary autoscaler override to respond to queue_depth spikes with a lower smoothing window for the affected pool. - Asked customer to stagger concurrent short streams where possible. Next steps: - SRE to propose autoscaler tuning: introduce a short-window microburst detector and a burst_reservation policy. - Platform to add telemetry for short-lived stream counts and slot fragmentation metric. - Product/SDK team to publish best-practice guidance to customers about connection reuse and batching short streams.

title: Dedicated pool throughput drop during quiet windows; transient GPU thinness traced to sidecar burst spike
preview:
Issue summary:
- Customer (QuillSync) reports periodic dedicated-pool throughput drop during their midday quiet windows (low traffic), recurring over 3 days. Affected routes are pinned to dedicated pool in us-west-2.
Impact:
- P50/P95 throughput decreased by ~30% for 2-6 minute windows; increased request queueing and observed fallback to shared tier for a subset of requests. Customer-facing latency spikes and elevated 429/504 retries.
Environment:
- prod:us-west-2, dedicated pool: quillsync-prod-pool-1, models: redwood/open-13b and redwood/opt-6.7b, customer_tier: dedicated.
Steps to reproduce:
1) Traffic profile: send steady low-rate traffic mimicking QuillSync background sync (10-15 concurrent short streams).
2) Wait for scheduled telemetry window (~midday, customer local timezone).
3) Observe allocator/gpu attach logs and autoscaler events for pool quillsync-prod-pool-1.
Observed behavior:
- Short windows of GPU availability thinness (slots reduce), autoscaler does not immediately scale up, queueing increases, routing starts falling back or throttling routes.
- Sidecar metrics show large short-lived burst of connections/metrics flushes coincident with drop windows.
Expected behavior:
- Dedicated pool should maintain steady attach capacity for pinned routes through quiet windows; autoscaler should respond within configured cooldowns to legitimate load shifts.
Initial pointers and logs:
- Customer-provided request traces: trace IDs attached in comments.
- SRE dashboard snapshot: p50 throughput chart and allocator-attach-count for quillsync-prod-pool-1 during 2026-03-11 to 2026-03-13.
- Sidecar metrics: telemetry-flush-rate spiked for multiple tenants at the same timestamps.

title: Dedicated capacity uneven shard affinity causing GPU starvation and autoscaler oscillation during off-peak window
preview:
Issue summary: NovusHealth observed an intermittent but sustained p50/p95 throughput degradation in their dedicated pool during a nightly off-peak window (approx 02:00-04:30 UTC). Requests queue and remain pending for several seconds despite spare GPU capacity reported across the cluster. Impact: customer-facing latency regressions and elevated queue lengths for a production model pinned to redwood-open-7b-v2. Environment: Dedicated reserved pool (pool-id: p-7461), region us-west-2, model pinned to redwood-open-7b-v2, customer uses small streaming requests (avg tokens/output small).

Observed behavior: autoscaler repeatedly scales down and back up during the window (oscillation) with short lived GPU attach/detach events. Several GPUs appear underutilized while others are saturated (high NVLink/PCIe stalls). Allocator logs show placement-group affinity enforcement that groups long-lived connections onto the same GPU shards. Concurrently, we observe a high proportion of small, long-lived streams from the customer (50-200ms per stream) that serialize into tiny batches and prevent effective packing. Expected behavior: autoscaler should maintain steady capacity with minimal oscillation and scheduler should evenly balance affinity/shards to avoid localized starvation.

Why this is unique: the pattern is not a classic noisy-tenant burst – it's a fragmentation effect caused by affinity placement plus many small, long-lived streams which prevents the batcher and KV-cache from coalescing requests. This leads to allocation fragmentation and localized NVLink/PCIe contention even with nominal free GPU slots.

Relevant timestamps: first alert 2026-03-12T02:07:21Z, repeat window 02:00–04:30 UTC nightly, mitigations applied 2026-03-13 03:10Z.

title: Microphone fails to enumerate after BIOS flash + Secure Boot toggle during depot repair
preview:
Issue summary: After a standard depot BIOS flash followed by toggling Secure Boot to re-enable legacy imaging paths, attached device microphones (internal array) no longer enumerate in Windows. Observed on small subset of fleet following recent vendor BIOS (v1.18.4) rollout to depot units.

Impact: Affected users see "No audio input device installed" in Settings and Device Manager; internal microphone missing from audio input list. External USB microphones still function. This blocks users from joining voice calls and impacts QA signoff for a subset of refurbished laptops. Approximately 7 confirmed units to date, all processed through the same depot workflow.

Environment: Depot repair/flash workflow, corp provisioning image (Win11-23H2-corp), Secure Boot toggled from Enabled -> Disabled -> Enabled during repair validation; BIOS version 1.18.4 on affected units.

Observed behavior: After reboot, Windows audio subsystem loads but Microphone Array device does not appear in Device Manager; no driver nodes for the audio DSP. Windows Event Log shows Service Control Manager entries for audioendpointbuilder failing to start dependent driver with Code 7000. Kernel logs show ACPI _DSM calls returning different hardware IDs after Secure Boot toggle.

Expected behavior: Internal microphone enumerates and appropriate audio codec driver (vendor) is present under Sound, video and game controllers and Audio inputs and outputs.

Notes: This ticket is an internal support/depot-repair workflow issue; not customer-facing. Triage requested to determine whether the BIOS change altered firmware visibility or audio codec microcontroller (DSP) firmware got re-flashed/disabled when Secure Boot state changed.

title: Okta SSO login triggers captive-portal detection which drops VPN and causes authentication loop for remote Wi‑Fi users
preview:
Issue summary: Several employees connecting from customer sites or hotel Wi‑Fi report that attempting to authenticate to internal apps via Okta SAML redirects triggers a captive‑portal flow. When users complete captive‑portal sign‑in, the VPN client (WireGuard-based corporate agent) detects the network change and tears down the tunnel, which invalidates the SSO session and returns the user to the captive portal — creating a loop.

Impact: Multiple remote employees (mostly traveling sales and solutions engineers) are unable to access internal dashboards or Atlassian tools until they fully disconnect and re‑authenticate. Affected users temporarily lose access to internal services for ~10–30 minutes. No customer‑facing systems impacted; internal productivity degradation.

Environment: macOS and Windows corporate images, Redwood VPN agent v2.9.1 (WireGuard wrapper), Okta SAML SSO, typical captive portal networks (hotel, conference Wi‑Fi), region: global (most reports from APAC/EMEA travel).

Observed behavior: SSO redirect to okta.company.com triggers captive‑portal HTTP/302 to the provider portal; the VPN agent sees an interface change and initiates rekey/reenroll which tears down existing sockets. Okta session cookie becomes invalid after the rebind and the user is presented with the captive portal again.

Expected behavior: Completing captive‑portal flow should retain network connection and VPN agent should gracefully wait for interface stabilization or pause rekey until user login completes so SSO exchange can finish.

Temporary workaround: Users must disconnect VPN, complete captive portal authentication, then re‑start VPN and re‑login to Okta. Recommended to perform captive‑portal sign‑in in a browser before starting VPN where possible.

title: Session linkage token mismatch blocks cross-account impersonation for support tooling
preview:
Issue summary: Since 2026-03-12 09:10 UTC, support tooling (impersonation flows used by oncall engineers via the internal console and CLI) intermittently fail with a 'session token mismatch' error when initiating cross-account impersonation. Impact: Oncall engineers cannot assume customer-facing service accounts for 5-30% of impersonation attempts, blocking urgent runbook actions and escalations. Environment: production us-east, identity-proxy v3.4.1, okta-device-trust enabled for privileged flows.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Login to console as admin (device trust approved)
2) Open 'Switch account / Impersonate' dialog and select external-tenant service-account
3) Click 'Assume role', or run 'rwd auth impersonate --account <tenant-id>' via CLI
4) Observe immediate failure in UI or CLI with error code 403/SESSION_LINK_MISMATCH and short correlation id in logs

Observed behavior: The identity-proxy returns 403 with message 'session_linkage_token invalid for target session'. The console shows a transient toast 'Unable to start impersonation — session verification failed'. CLI returns non-zero exit and logs: 'auth: session_linkage: verification failed: token not in session map'.

Expected behavior: Token exchange should validate and produce a scoped session for the target account or fall back to a clear diagnostic flow requesting reauthorization.

Investigation notes:
- Correlation IDs collected from affected attempts: rwd-imp-3f9b, rwd-imp-3fa0, rwd-cli-4b12. Logs attached to ticket in the support bucket (s3://redwood-support/logs/impersonation/2026-03-12/).
- Identity-proxy logs show the incoming assertion includes a linkage_token header (base64 string). The session-store (redis cluster auth-sessions-prod) lookup for the linkage token intermittently returns a 5xx from the replica nodes leading to token not found. The failure window aligns with a 2-minute rolling restart of redis-read replicas (maintenance job kicked off at 09:09 UTC by infra auto-heal).
- Reproduced locally by toggling a TTL skew: if session-store read misses then the verification fails immediately with the same error.
- No Okta assertion failures observed; device-trust asserts ok.
- Related code paths: identity-proxy handler verifySessionLinkage(), session-cache middleware, rwd-auth-cli token exchange.

Temporary mitigation:
- Oncall instructed to retry impersonation (most retries succeed). A short-term runbook added to the support-confluence page.
- Infra team paused the rolling restart and warmed read replicas; observed reduction in failures.

Next steps / Proposed fix:
1) Add a robust retry layer to session-store reads in identity-proxy (backoff + failover to primary) — PR proposed: https://github.com/redwood-inference/identity-proxy/pull/1872
2) Improve observability: emit metrics for session-linkage cache misses and 5xx on redis replicas. Dashboard ticket created under MON-impersonation-calls.
3) Add defensive behavior: if linkage token not found but assertion from Okta valid, present clear UI guidance to reauthorize rather than generic 403.

References: Slack thread #support-impersonation (https://slack.redwoodinference/support-impersonation/p12345), PR above, redis maintenance logs (infra ticket INT-931200).

2) Improve observability: emit metrics for session-linkage cache misses and 5xx on redis replicas. Dashboard ticket created under MON-impersonation-calls.
3) Add defensive behavior: if linkage token not found but assertion from Okta valid, present clear UI guidance to reauthorize rather than generic 403.

References: Slack thread #support-impersonation (https://slack.redwoodinference/support-impersonation/p12345), PR above, redis maintenance logs (infra ticket INT-931200).

title: Intermittent tail-drain timeouts for very large max_tokens / long-prefix streaming requests
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers are observing intermittent streaming disconnects and finalization timeouts when submitting requests with very large max_tokens (>=150k) and extended conversation history (many system/user messages). The generation runs for tens of seconds and then the client receives a 504 or streaming connection is closed without final chunk. This appears to correlate with sustained long-token drains and sudden KV cache thrashing on Dedicated pools.

Impact:
- Affected customer (NoteChat Inc., dedicated) reports ~10% of long-run streaming completions fail to finalize, causing truncated transcripts and user-visible errors.
- Business impact: production feature degradation for premium customers; customers are throttling long-context usage.

Environment:
- Dedicated GPU pool (g4dn-like), prod-us-east, isolated tenant.
- Requests routed to serving runtime version v1.48.0 with batching enabled and default cache policy.

Observed behavior:
- Streaming starts normally; progress chunks arrive for long periods. Around 30-90s into generation the stream stalls and then client sees a 504 or socket closed. Server logs show finalizer timeout and worker marked as overloaded then request requeued and dropped.

Expected behavior:
- Long-generation requests should stream to completion (within documented SLO) or be failed with graceful error that includes diagnostic id.

Logs / traces / evidence:
- Trace IDs: trace-us-east-20260310-7b3f, trace-us-east-20260311-c9a1.
- SRE dashboard: spike in finalizer-queue-time and kv-cache-evict rate between 03/10 11:12–11:30 UTC.
- Router logs show transient priority-queue backpressure and worker-level contention.

Steps to reproduce (customer repro):
1) Create a conversation with ~40 short turn messages (system + user tokens ~70k) as prefix.
2) Submit a single streaming generation with max_tokens set to 160000 and temperature 0.7 using model redwood-3b-quant.
3) Observe streaming chunks for ~20–60s, then either final chunk missing or 504 returned.

Workaround attempted by customer:
- Reducing max_tokens to 80k reduces occurrences to <1% but limits UX.
- Pinning to a non-quantized variant (llama-2-13b-instruct) changed failure mode to increased latency but fewer finalizer timeouts.
- Disabling client-side streaming and polling for full response caused same server-side timeout after ~2 minutes.

title: API Gateway 5xx amplification from connection-slab fragmentation and scheduler latency during tenant burst
preview:
Issue summary: NoteStream reported a rapid increase in 5xx responses (502/503/504) beginning 2026-03-12 18:32 UTC during a scheduled bulk ingestion. Impact: production traffic for NoteStream (dedicated pool) experienced elevated error rates (~18% 5xx for 20 minutes) and increased tail latency causing customer throttles and several client timeouts. Environment: prod, us-east, dedicated GPU pool.

Observed behavior: API Gateway began returning 502/503 in waves correlated with spikes in connection queue length and a burst of ephemeral worker churn. The serving runtime reported repeated short-lived OOMs and scheduler logs show latency spikes in task assignment. KV-cache metrics show increased shard contention and growth of pending prefetch queues.

Expected behavior: Traffic should remain within acceptable error floor (<0.1% 5xx) using the tenant's reserved capacity and fallback routing should continue serving requests with minimal errors.

Steps to reproduce (approximate):
1) Customer triggered a large multi-tenanted embedding upload (~1.2M vectors) which hit the dedicated ingestion route.
2) Traffic pattern: sustained 1000 rps with occasional 5k rps bursts over 10s windows.
3) Within 1-2 minutes of bursts, api-gateway accept queue grows, keepalive pool exhibits high turnover, and runtime scheduler delays tasks > 250ms.

Logs / dashboards referenced:
- Support dashboard (internal): /dashboards/incidents/SUP-404321
- Grafana: apigw/conn-queue-length (spike), scheduler/assignment-latency (tail spike), runtime/gpu-memory-alloc-failures (brief increases).
- Customer-provided client logs: multiple HTTP 502 with upstream-reset messages and intermittent TLS handshake failures.

Immediate customer impact: degraded UX for NoteStream embedding uploads; retries caused higher load and amplified the condition.

title: Priority inversion in continuous batcher causing tenant-specific p99 latency spikes
preview:
Issue summary: Starting 2026-03-12 ~09:10 UTC, NimbusPay reported sharp p95/p99 latency regressions for inference requests routed to their Dedicated pool in us-east. The regression appears correlated with recent continuous-batcher scheduler change that introduced fairness/priority weighting. Impact: p95 increased from ~85ms baseline to ~720ms and p99 up to 4.2s for NimbusPay tenant traffic; production user-facing timeouts (504s) rose from 0.01% to 0.9% for the affected endpoints. Not all tenants are affected; behavior seems tenant-specific and workload-sensitive (mixed short and long sequences). Environment: prod (us-east), dedicated capacity pool rn-dedicated-us-east-12 serving gpt-3.5-redwood-v1. Observed windows: 09:05–09:55 UTC. This is customer-impacting and on a paid dedicated tenant.

Immediate customer-visible symptoms: increased request latency tails, occasional 504 timeouts, and perceived instability of streaming sessions. Customer reported behavior after they rolled a mixed-length workload test (short prompts for routing logic + long generation for summaries).

title: Repeated 429 waves after scale-up: HTTP/2 multiplex and token-refill skew on Dedicated pool
preview:
Issue summary:
BrightCart began seeing recurring 429 waves across multiple services immediately after a Dedicated pool scale-up event at ~2026-03-10T02:10:00Z. The waves manifest as clustered 429 responses for ~3-8 minutes following host additions and then subside, only to reappear on subsequent scale events. Impact: production customers experienced degraded throughput and failed upload jobs (embeds + short chat requests) for the duration of the waves.

Impact:
- Several background bulk-embed jobs failed and retried concurrently (customer reported increase in error rate from 0.2% -> 4.6%).
- Interactive chat latency spiked and some calls returned 429s, affecting UI experience for a subset of users (approx 3-5% of active sessions).

Environment:
- Dedicated pool (customer-owned reservation) in prod-us-east (us-east-1)
- API access over multiplexed HTTP/2 (gRPC-web) via customer edge proxy
- Autoscaler added 6 hosts during the observed window (scale-up from 12 -> 18 GPUs)

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern):
1) Start sustained mixed workload: N = 400 concurrent clients (mix of short chat + batch embed streams).
2) Trigger autoscaler policy by gradually increasing throughput for ~10 minutes.
3) Observe scale-up event and host join logs in autoscaler.
4) Immediately after host join, see clustered 429s on a subset of client connections for 3-8 minutes; then normal traffic resumes.

Observed behavior:
- 429 responses include X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0 and Retry-After headers of varying values (30-90s).
- Throttle metrics show per-connection token buckets being depleted quickly; refill counters on newly joined hosts are offset relative to origin.
- HTTP/2 connection reuse patterns concentrate many logical streams onto a few long-lived connections; when a new host joins, edge-to-origin affinity changes and tokens are not rebalanced smoothly.

Expected behavior:
- After scale-up the system should rebalance load without producing transient tenant-wide 429 waves; tokens/refill epochs should remain aligned and credits should not be double-counted or lost during host join.

Logs / quick traces (redacted):
- api-gateway: 2026-03-10T02:12:18Z WARN ingress: "token-window-mismatch detected for api_key=acct-brightcart-44" host_join_id=host-17
- rate-limiter: 2026-03-10T02:12:19Z INFO refill: "epoch_offset=+42s" host=host-17 tenant=acct-brightcart-44
- ingress-proxy: 2026-03-10T02:13:02Z ERROR connection: "http2 stream reset after 429 flood" session_id=svc-8fc

Additional context:
- Customer uses a pool of long-lived HTTP/2 connections through a caching edge proxy; connections are sticky to a small set of origin hosts for affinity.
- Customer reported they recently changed client-side connection pooling to hold connections open for longer (to reduce handshake overhead).

Customer request:
- BrightCart asks for guidance and a fix path to prevent these recurring 429 waves during future scale events and for an explanation of root cause.

title: Delayed TTFB and 504s for long-sequence completions caused by ALB keepalive churn + proxy buffering
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers running long-generation requests (2000+ token completions) intermittently see very large time-to-first-byte (TTFB) or final 504s despite backend logs indicating model started generating. Impact: several enterprise customers on Dedicated experienced increased error rates and perceived performance regressions since 2026-03-07.

Impact:
- LexaDocs reported ~8% error rate on long completions; user-facing slow requests and failed job retries.
- Symptom pattern: request accepted, no bytes for 8–30s, then ALB or gateway returns 504 or connection reset. In other cases first bytes arrive but final completion truncated.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool (us-east-1) behind ALB -> nginx sidecar -> serving runtime.
- Large tracing headers and cookie payloads (custom CSRF + jwt rotation) present for affected customers.

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1) Send a streaming generate with prompt resulting in >2000 token completion and long KV-cache warmup on cold path.
2) Include a 12–18KB X-Trace header and persistent cookie set.
3) Observe TTFB spike 10–25s on ~1/20 attempts; ALB logs frequently show Target: Connection closed by client or idle timeout.

Attachments / artifacts:
- Relevant ALB access logs: s3://redwood-logs/alb/2026/03/07/lexadocs.log.gz
- Nginx sidecar error logs (snippets in comments)
- Serving-runtime traces: trace ids 20260309-lexa-01..05

Initial hypothesis:
- ALB connection reuse/keepalive churn + large headers cause HPACK fragmentation and reorder; nginx proxy buffering combined with no bytes from model early (KV cold path) results in ALB treating the connection idle and closing it (ALB idle timeout ~15s).
- Proxy buffering rules can delay flushing of headers/body until buffer thresholds met, making TTFB appear larger.

Urgency and customer impact:
High — enterprise customers seeing production errors. Workaround requested while we investigate permanent fixes.

title: Sustained throughput drop on dedicated GPU pool due to autoscaler undershoot and cross-tenant GPU contention
preview:
Issue summary:
Sustained degradation in tokens/sec and increased 5xx/timeouts for tenant 'AcmeAI' on their dedicated pool (pool-id: pool-usw-41). Starts ~2026-03-10 02:15 UTC and persisted despite autoscaler activity.

Impact:
- Production inference latency increased from 60-80ms p50 to 900-2,500ms p50/p95 for typical chat requests.
- Request timeouts (gateway 504) and queueing observed. Affected approximately 20% of traffic for the pool; customer reported user-visible failures in multiple services.

Environment:
- Deployment: Dedicated pool pool-usw-41 (US-West-2)
- Region: us-west-2
- Customer tier: dedicated
- Models in use: redwood-v2-large (primary), llm-13b-quant (fallback)
- Components engaged: dedicated-pool, autoscaler, scheduler, serving-runtime, api-gateway

Steps to reproduce (from load test):
1) Run sustained ramp to 350 concurrent requests over 30 minutes against redwood-v2-large route.
2) Observe tokens/sec drop at ~120 concurrent requests and growing request queue.
3) Increase to 420 concurrent requests; autoscaler triggers but pod additions lag >90s and throughput stays low.
4) Re-run after forcing min_replicas=4 and scale-up cooldown reduced: throughput recovers.

Observed metrics and logs:
- GPU utilization per-node: 98-100% on 2 nodes, 20-40% on newly added nodes.
- GPU memory fragmentation spikes; OOM kill events at kernel level in serving-runtime logs.
- Autoscaler events: frequent scale up requests at 02:18 and 02:21 UTC but only 1 new replica added due to cooldown and allocation constraints.
- Queue depth alerts: queue_depth > 800 requests for 7 minutes.
- API gateway logs: 504 timeouts correlated with peak queue depth. Sample log line: "2026-03-10T02:19:12Z ERROR gateway request_id=... status=504 latency_ms=30000"

Hypothesis:
1) Autoscaler configuration has undersized min_replicas and long cooldown which causes delayed capacity addition (autoscale undershoot).
2) One node in the pool is saturated by a noisy tenant job (high-intensity batched throughput) causing GPU contention; scheduler did not rebalance quickly because of allocation constraints.
3) Serving runtime encountering memory fragmentation with the current quantization path for llm-13b-quant under certain sequence lengths leading to elevated kernel times.

Immediate mitigation taken:
- Forced min_replicas from 2 -> 4 for pool-usw-41 at 2026-03-10T03:02 UTC; observed tokens/sec recovery within 60-90s.
- Drained the suspected noisy node and cordoned it for investigation (node-id: g4-usw-node-117).
- Temporarily disabled mixed-batch allocation for redwood-v2-large on the pool to reduce intra-node contention.

Next steps and action items:
- Engineering to patch autoscaler: reduce scale_up cooldown from 60s -> 20s and adjust target queue depth thresholds. (PR created)
- Add per-node tenant isolation signals to scheduler to avoid mixing heavy batch jobs with latency-sensitive routes.
- Run stress tests across sequence length distributions to reproduce memory fragmentation; optimize serving-runtime kernel selection for affected quant paths.
- Add alerting rule for sustained queue_depth > 200 for > 2m and GPUs with 90%+ utilization for >1m.

Attachments provided:
- Grafana snapshots: URL(s) included in linked_confluence page.
- Gateway logs sample and serving-runtime logs attached to ticket.

- Forced min_replicas from 2 -> 4 for pool-usw-41 at 2026-03-10T03:02 UTC; observed tokens/sec recovery within 60-90s.
- Drained the suspected noisy node and cordoned it for investigation (node-id: g4-usw-node-117).
- Temporarily disabled mixed-batch allocation for redwood-v2-large on the pool to reduce intra-node contention.

Next steps and action items:
- Engineering to patch autoscaler: reduce scale_up cooldown from 60s -> 20s and adjust target queue depth thresholds. (PR created)
- Add per-node tenant isolation signals to scheduler to avoid mixing heavy batch jobs with latency-sensitive routes.
- Run stress tests across sequence length distributions to reproduce memory fragmentation; optimize serving-runtime kernel selection for affected quant paths.
- Add alerting rule for sustained queue_depth > 200 for > 2m and GPUs with 90%+ utilization for >1m.

Attachments provided:
- Grafana snapshots: URL(s) included in linked_confluence page.
- Gateway logs sample and serving-runtime logs attached to ticket.

title: Streaming session stalls then emits duplicated partial tokens after keepalive refill behind CDN
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer Fetchfolio reports that long-running streaming responses (SSE and WS) intermittently pause for ~6-12s and then resume with duplicated partial token sequences. This occurs when connections traverse the customer's CloudFront distribution in front of our edge and only for sessions that had keepalive refresh traffic from the CDN.

Impact:
- Affects production traffic for a single dedicated tenant (Fetchfolio).
- Stalls cause UI timeouts and duplicate partial tokens which confuse the client's incremental-merge logic (app shows duplicated phrases).
- Reproducible ~1-2x / 1000 streams during peak traffic windows.

Environment:
- Customer dedicated deployment routed through CloudFront -> Redwood edge LB -> api-gateway -> streaming-proxy -> serving-runtime.
- Region: us-east.
- Models: redwood-1-large, openlm-13b-v2.

Steps to reproduce (from customer):
1. Start long prompt generation with streaming=true and client-side incremental merge enabled.
2. Route traffic through customer's CloudFront (default caching + keepalive enabled).
3. Observe mid-session pause (6-12s), followed by resumed chunks where a sequence of tokens repeats a ~10-30 token window.

Observed logs/headers (sanitized):
- Upstream keepalive refresh: CF-Idle-Timeout: 30s; X-Edge-Refill: true
- Edge logs show connection handoff followed by a 200-response chunk gap timestamped at 6s
- Serving runtime shows no CPU/GPU saturation and continuous generation; KV cache metrics show expected hits.

Additional notes:
- Customer provided example trace id: trace-fet-20260312-07
- Client increments merge by token offsets; duplicated tokens lead to visible repeated phrases in their web UI.
- Happens on both SSE and WS but frequency higher on SSE when CloudFront keepalive is active.

title: KV-cache growth causing oversized batch formation and transient GPU memory pressure in dedicated pool
preview:
Issue summary:
ByteLex started seeing sustained p99 latency increases and intermittent 5xxs on dedicated routes backed by redwood/gptx-13b starting 2026-03-11 21:00 UTC. Impact: multiple customer endpoints degraded for ~2 hours with requests being retried and observed token throughput drop.

Impact:
- Elevated p99 latency from ~300ms to >2.5s on dedicated tenant routes.
- Increased request queueing and occasional OOM-like GPU allocation failures on node-level metrics.
- Customer-facing errors (5xx) and auto-retries causing higher token counts and billing noise.

Environment:
- Deployment: Dedicated pool (ByteLex-reserved) in us-east-1.
- Runtime: serving-runtime v2.9.1 + kv-cache service v1.4.
- Models: redwood/gptx-13b (quantized) and fallback gpt-j-6b.

Steps to reproduce (from SRE repro run):
1) Replay ByteLex production traffic capture (short average context, many short streams).
2) Simulate high rate of prefix-heavy requests that reuse cached KV but with many low-entropy prompts.
3) Observe KV-cache size growth per tenant and larger-than-expected batch aggregation.

Observed telemetry / logs:
- KV-cache resident set increased by ~3.8x for the tenant over 30 minutes (kv-cache/exporter metrics).
- Batch assembler metric \"average_batch_size\" increased from 8 -> 38 for short-stream routes.
- GPU memory usage p95 increased to 96% on 3/6 nodes in the dedicated pool with transient allocation failures logged: \"cudaMalloc failed: out of memory\" (node-agent logs).
- Autoscaler reacted by removing a single small node then re-adding; observed autoscale oscillation window ~5min.
- No explicit preemption events in cloud provider logs; underlying nodes remained.

Hypothesis (investigation so far):
- KV-cache growth for heavily prefix-reused but low-entropy short streams leads to unexpectedly large per-request state being retained; when batch assembler groups many such short streams, effective memory per batch balloons causing sudden GPU memory pressure.
- Autoscaler sees temporary memory-pressure signals and scales down (evicting fractional capacity) then rapidly scales back up, producing oscillation and temporary capacity gaps.
- Noisy neighboring tenants with intermittent long-context replay exacerbate memory usage and make the stateful memory signals noisy, confusing autoscaler heuristics.

Temporary mitigations tested:
- Disabled aggressive batch-inflation heuristic for ByteLex routes (runtime flag) — resulted in immediate drop of avg_batch_size to ~10 and stabilized GPU memory.
- Evicted a small subset of KV-cache entries via targeted eviction API which reduced resident size ~40% and alleviated OOMs.

Next steps and action items:
- SRE to roll a config change to back off batch inflation heuristics for all dedicated pools with high short-stream ratios (PR in progress: runtime/pull/4932).
- Engineering (kv-cache) to add per-tenant cap enforcement and better eviction scoring to prevent unbounded growth (kv-cache/pull/118).
- Autoscaler owners to add hysteresis on memory-driven downscales when previous scale event within 10 minutes (work item created).
- Run a controlled canary for the config change tonight at 02:00 UTC in a single us-east zone and monitor p99 and GPU OOMs.

Customer communication:
- Notified ByteLex via email and provided temporary workaround (disable batch inflation flag) at 2026-03-12 02:45 UTC. Customer accepted and saw immediate improvement.

References:
- Slack thread: #support (link: slack://channel/support/thread/joe-1122)
- Node logs: s3://redwood-logs/prod/us-east/bytes-lex/node-logs-2026-03-11.tar.gz

title: Service accounts lost Console/CI access after Okta group prefix remapping
preview: Issue summary: Several internal service accounts and a small set of CI pipelines started receiving 403/permission-denied errors when calling the Redwood Console API and internal admin endpoints immediately after an Okta group naming convention change was applied by Identity. Impact: 7 service accounts and 4 CI workflows failed auth checks; internal tooling and alerting UIs were inaccessible for owners. Environment: Production identity sync (SCIM) + Redwood Console RBAC mapping, us-east region. Timeline: group prefix remapping applied 2026-03-10 07:15 UTC, first failures observed 07:21 UTC. Expected: group membership mapping should preserve effective RBAC roles for service principals and bot users. Observed: group-based roles no longer resolved for SAML/SCIM-provisioned service accounts because Okta attribute used for mapping changed from groups.name to groups.displayName in the recent change.

title: SSO auth proxy header collision causes intermittent 401/403 for analytics gateway login
preview:
Issue summary:
Users and SREs reported intermittent 401/403 when logging into the analytics portal and when machine agents call the insights API from app-hosted flows. Errors began appearing after proxy middleware v2.14.0 was deployed to prod on 2026-03-09.

Impact:
- ~8% of analytics-portal login attempts fail during peak minutes (observed 09:00–11:00 PDT).
- Automated ingestion jobs that rely on service-to-service SSO occasionally fail with 401s, triggering retries and delayed dashboards.

Environment:
- Prod (us-west-2) behind identity-proxy fleet (k8s, istio sidecar + custom auth middleware).

Observed behavior:
- Requests that should be allowed by Okta SSO show a 401 from the analytics-gateway.
- When retries succeed, request headers show slightly different casing/order for auth-related headers.
- No broad Okta outage; only services routed through the updated proxy are affected.

First seen: 2026-03-09 08:54 PDT (alerts from support + SRE dashboards).
