title: Post-SSD repair: systemd-resume hang after hibernation prevents fleet enrollment and SCCM check-in on Ubuntu 22.04
preview:
Issue summary:
After SSD replacement and standard depot provisioning, several Ubuntu 22.04 laptops are failing to complete post-repair check-in. The devices appear to boot normally but never reach fleet enrollment (fleet-agent) and SCCM client check-in. Investigations show the machine is stuck in the systemd 'resume' target after a hibernation/resume cycle triggered by the depot hardware test.

Impact:
- Affected devices are not joining inventory or receiving policy, delaying redeployment to users.
- ~6 devices reported from depot on 2026-03-08 and 2026-03-09.
- Operational impact: rework and manual reprovisioning required for redeployments.

Environment:
- Ubuntu 22.04 LTS (custom corporate image built via imaging pipeline)
- kernel 5.15.0-80-generic (corp-pinned)
- Fleet-agent v1.8.2, SCCM agent shim for Linux v2.1.0
- SSD vendor: Western Digital SN730 (replacement units from depot pool)
- Boot mode: UEFI, Secure Boot enabled

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1) Depot engineer replaces NVMe SSD with stock SN730 unit and re-flashes corporate image via imaging station (PXE → task sequence).
2) Imaging completes and system runs hardware self-test which forces a suspend/hibernate cycle at end of task sequence (this is new in depot script rev 2026-02-28).
3) Machine resumes and reaches systemd resume.target but several services never transition to running (fleet-agent, sccm-shim).
4) Device appears responsive over serial/console, but fleet enroll never completes and SCCM check-in does not occur.

Observed logs / artifacts:
- journalctl -b shows repeated timeouts in systemd-resume.service and a backtrace for kernel resume_resume_device for NVMe (attached lines below).
- /var/log/fleet-agent.log ends with \"waiting for network\" but networking shows UP from ip link.
- dmesg shows \"nvme 0000:01:00.0: failed to resume queue 0\" intermittently.

Investigation notes:
- Hypothesis 1: NVMe driver resume path regression when using replacement SN730 units + current kernel interacting with corporate init ordering.
- Hypothesis 2: the depot's testing script forces a hibernate state that trips a systemd ordering edge case where resume.target occurs before network.target, causing fleet-agent's registration to wait indefinitely behind a masked wait.
- Reproduced on one lab device by explicitly triggering hibernate after imaging; same symptoms observed.
- Booting with \"noresume\" kernel param allows normal boot and fleet enrollment proceeds, indicating resume code path is implicated.
- Kernel upgrade test candidate (5.15.0-90) in staging reduced resume hang frequency but did not fully eliminate.
- Vendor NVMe firmware on affected replacement units is 1.0.3; depot stock contains mixed firmware versions.

Temporary workaround:
- Depot engineers should avoid triggering the hibernate step in the post-imaging script (reverted to previous script version 2026-02-21) until a fix is validated.
- For devices already affected: boot with kernel parameter \"noresume\" then re-trigger normal boot to allow fleet enrollment; technicians can then re-image if desired.

Next steps / action items:
- Imaging team to roll back depot script to pre-hibernate version (owner: Priya Shah, due 2026-03-12).
- Kernel team to validate whether kernel resume path fixes in 5.15.0-90/5.19 backports resolve the NVMe resume failures (owner: Ethan Park, in progress).
- Procurement/dev-ops to audit depot NVMe firmware and segregate SN730 units with firmware 1.0.3 (owner: Marco Li, due 2026-03-15).
- Create focused test matrix: SSD vendor/firmware x kernel version x hibernate enabled/disabled. Capture reproducibility matrix and logs (owner: Imaging QA).

Acceptance criteria for resolution:
- Depot imaging script can run with hibernate test and devices consistently resume and complete fleet enrollment without manual intervention.
- Root cause documented and either kernel patch, firmware update, or imaging change implemented.

title: NetworkManager writes duplicate default routes during imaging causing AAD join timeout in ap-southeast
preview:
Issue summary:
During automated task-sequence imaging in APAC (ap-southeast-1), NetworkManager on fresh Windows PE handoff writes two overlapping default routes (0.0.0.0/0) to the primary interface. The duplicated routes have different metrics and intermittently cause the Azure AD join step to time out (AADSTS network error) or attempt to use an internal management VPN endpoint, failing domain registration and stalling the task sequence.

Impact:
- ~5-8% of devices in the last 48h fail AAD join during imaging and are left in the 'Autopilot not completed' state.
- Affected devices require manual intervention (local admin join + re-run of post-provision scripts) delaying depot turn-around.
- Mostly impacts APAC staging fleet and a small subset of production depot images.

Environment:
- Imaging orchestrator: SCCM task sequence handing off to WinPE -> OOBE scripts (internal imaging-playbooks v3.7).
- Network manager: Custom NM shim used in WinPE handoff (win-nm-wrapper v0.9.2).
- Observed on Lenovo ThinkPad X1 Carbon Gen11, Dell Latitude 7440.
- Regions: ap-southeast-1 staging and select prod depots.

Steps to reproduce (intermittent):
1) Boot device into SCCM task sequence using depot network VLAN (APAC depot).
2) WinPE hands off to windows setup, imaging-playbook triggers win-nm-wrapper to apply DHCP/route config.
3) Watch netstat / route print or use PowerShell Get-NetRoute after handoff.
4) Observe two default routes: one with metric 15 and one with metric 5, sometimes bound to same interface.
5) Continue imaging -> Azure AD Join step times out or reports network unreachable.

Observed behavior:
- Duplicate default routes appear immediately after win-nm-wrapper runs; sometimes the lower-metric route points at a management VLAN gateway that should be unreachable from the depot VLAN.
- AAD join logs show network requests routed to the wrong gateway and either timing out or returning HTTP 502 from an internal proxy.
- Re-running networking script that deletes the duplicate route allows AAD join to succeed.

Expected behavior:
- Single default route to depot gateway (correct metric).
- AAD join uses depot network path to Microsoft endpoints.

Logs / Artifacts attached in ticket: list of sample Get-NetRoute outputs, win-nm-wrapper logs, SCCM task sequence trace snippets, sample AAD join traces (correlation IDs) and pcap from one repro.

Investigation notes:
- Initial hypothesis: race between DHCP client registering routes and win-nm-wrapper adding explicit route entries leads to duplicate entries.
- win-nm-wrapper v0.9.2 introduced a change to add a default-route fallback with lower metric when DHCP lease is delayed (PR infra/imaging-playbooks#157). Suspect this path improperly fires even when DHCP later populates the route.
- Correlated failures with depot switches that have intermittent DHCP lease latency (~100-400ms) during peak provisioning windows.
- Reproduced locally by delaying DHCP response in a lab: when wrapper adds fallback route and DHCP later adds its route, system ends up with two default routes. Windows route selection uses metric so traffic can go to unintended gateway.

Temporary workaround:
- Add a cleanup step in task sequence immediately after win-nm-wrapper: run PowerShell to remove duplicate default routes, keeping the DHCP-provided one (script snippet available). Deployed to staging depots, reduced failures to ~0.5% in first 12h.

Next steps / action items:
- Engineering to patch win-nm-wrapper to check for an existing default route and avoid adding fallback when DHCP is expected (owner: infra/imaging team).
- Long-term: update imaging-playbooks to wait for DHCP lease or implement an idempotent route reconcile step; add unit test for delayed DHCP scenarios.
- Ops: monitor APAC depot provisioning metrics and roll the cleanup script to remaining depots pending patch.

Resolution (work in progress):
- Temporary cleanup script deployed to 2 staging depots -- effective as a stopgap.
- Patch PR opened: infra/imaging-playbooks#157 (fix adds route-existence check and 2s wait for DHCP). Pending code review and canary.

title: Clarify provisional license reclaim window and equipment-return grace period for immediate offboarding
preview:
Issue summary:
HR received reports from Finance and IT that when an employee is placed on Immediate Termination (ITerm) in Workday, the seat/license in third-party SaaS (notably the design/legal tool vendor) remains billable for up to 14 days and the assigned corporate laptop sometimes remains tagged as 'active' in our asset management inventory. This ticket seeks clarification of the intended policy (grace windows) and the operational mechanics (which systems enforce the reclaim/return windows).

Impact:
- Potential short-term double-billing for SaaS seats when seats are quickly re-assigned.
- Delays in device recovery leading to inventory inaccuracies and security exposure for high-risk departures.
- Confusion between HR, IT, and Finance on expected timelines and required manual steps.

Environment:
- Identity sync: Okta -> SCIM -> downstream SaaS (prod)
- Asset tracking: FleetOps (prod) with inventory sync to ITSM
- HR system: Workday (prod)

Observed behavior / Steps to reproduce:
1) Create an employee termination in Workday with 'Immediate' flag at 09:00 UTC.
2) Workday emits termination event, HR webhook updates Okta.
3) Okta SCIM provisioning sends deactivation to SaaS vendor; we observe the user status change but vendor maintains seat as billable for a 14-day grace window (vendor policy).
4) FleetOps does not mark the laptop as 'awaiting pickup' until an asset-handback task is created manually by IT; automatic tagging is not triggered by the termination event.
5) Finance reports a new invoice line for an additional seat 3 days later after license reassign because the old seat was not reclaimed yet.

Logs / evidence: 
- Okta SCIM audit (okta-audit-2026-03-09.log) shows deactivation event at 09:02 UTC and response 200 from vendor with response body: {"status":"deprovision-scheduled","grace_days":14}.
- FleetOps event stream shows no asset lifecycle change tied to the Workday termination event (fleetops-events-2026-03-09.ndjson).
- Finance ticket FIN-1189 referencing unexpected seat charge for week of 2026-03-02.

Question / Requested clarification:
- What are the company-wide defaults for SaaS license reclaim windows and how many days are considered 'standard' vs 'exception' requiring manual immediate reclaim?
- For Immediate Terminations, do we have an established exception/accelerated flow that forces vendor-side seat revocation and device pickup tagging?
- Which teams are expected to take manual actions vs which actions should be automated by identity/asset tooling?

Desired outcome:
- A concise policy statement HR/IT/Finance can rely on for immediate-term offboards, including: default reclaim window, criteria for accelerating reclaim, and the single authoritative runbook link for immediate offboards.

title: Partial-stream resume replay leads to duplicate external webhook side-effects for Python function-calls
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports duplicate external API calls (webhook POSTs) when using Python SDK streaming + function-calling. Behavior occurs after a network interruption causes the client to reconnect and resume a partially delivered streaming response. The resumed session appears to replay the function invocation without the original idempotency token reaching the downstream webhook, resulting in side-effect duplication (duplicate invoices / duplicate task creation).

Impact:
- Customer (SlateCRM Inc.) is observing duplicate third-party webhook side-effects impacting billing and downstream task systems.
- Occurs intermittently on long-running streaming interactions where the client reconnects to resume.
- Business impact: customer flagged as P1 due to operational impact on billing pipeline.

Environment:
- Redwood Dedicated (customer-owned VPC) in us-east region.
- SDK: python-sdk v1.8.7 pinned by the integration.
- Server: streaming-proxy v2.12.
- Webhook consumer is a third-party billing service that relies on a header idempotency-key to dedupe requests.

Desired outcome:
- Prevent duplicated external side-effects on stream resume by ensuring idempotency metadata persists across reconnect/resume flows, or provide a recommended workaround pattern for safe function execution from streaming clients.

title: Intermittent p99 timeouts after client stream restarts due to continuous-batcher linger accumulation
preview:
Issue summary: Customers (NimbleRetail dedicated tenancy) observed intermittent p99 user-facing timeouts (504 / request timeouts) concentrated after client-side stream restarts. Impact: Multiple production routes serving chat completions saw p95->p99 divergence, with p99 climbing from ~450ms baseline to 3-6s and occasional 504s. Affected customer reports started 2026-03-10 14:20 UTC and continued in waves.

Environment: prod (us-east), dedicated pool tenant for NimbleRetail, models: redwood-1.0-mk4 (default), fallback to gptj-14b configured.

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern):
1) Client opens a streaming connection and makes a short request (~12 tokens).
2) Client intentionally restarts the stream shortly after (~300-800ms) to refresh headers/session.
3) Server continuous-batcher observed many short-lived streams that resume quickly; linger timers accumulate pending micro-batches.
4) After a burst of restarts, batch flushes become interleaved and inflate processing latency for a subset of requests, producing p99 spikes and eventual client timeouts.

Observed behavior: Periodic clustering of very short-lived streams leads to "linger buildup" inside continuous-batcher. Instead of flushing quickly, micro-batches coalesce across restart boundaries and wait for stray tokens/kv-cache warmups, creating head-of-line blocking for otherwise short requests. Metrics show increased batch sizes for affected cohorts paired with elevated per-request scheduling delay.

Expected behavior: Short-lived resumed streams should either be routed to a fast-path (low-linger flush) or isolated so that stream restart churn does not elevate latency for unrelated short requests.

Logs/metrics:
- Request traces: https://grafana.redwood/company/d/streams/stream-traces?var-tenant=nimbleretail&from=2026-03-10T14:15:00Z&to=2026-03-10T15:30:00Z
- SLO alerts: #incidents in Slack: https://slack.redwood.com/archives/C05SUPPORT/p1646990000
- Example backend traces (zip): s3://redwood-support-dumps/SUP-135790/traces-2026-03-10.zip
- Notable log excerpt: "batcher: linger accumulated 42ms > configured20ms; flush delayed by kv-cache-warmup(1)" repeated across affected shards.

Investigation notes:
- Reproduced locally by running a client harness that repeatedly opens and closes streams with 200-700ms gap; showed same pattern of increased scheduling delay and larger-than-expected micro-batches.
- Continuous-batcher metrics show a drift in per-cohort linger window when there are many rapid stream lifecycle events; the cohort state machine was leaving stale timers active.
- KV-cache miss/warmup interactions: when a resumed stream is first routed to a node with cold KV cache, the batcher delays flush waiting for cache population which can exceed linger threshold for other coalesced requests.
- Autoscaling and GPU utilization looked healthy; not a capacity shortage.

Root-cause hypothesis: An interaction between stream lifecycle events (frequent restarts), cohort timer state, and kv-cache warmup causes the continuous-batcher to extend effective linger windows for a short period. That extended linger allows otherwise short requests to be coalesced into larger micro-batches or wait on cache-population, causing p99 latency spikes and some timeouts.

Immediate mitigations tested:
- Toggle: applied a config override for NimbleRetail tenant to reduce continuous-batcher linger max to 8ms (from 20ms) for affected routes; saw p99 drop back toward baseline in canary cluster.
- Enabled per-connection fast-path for short-lived streams (experimental) which bypasses cohort coalescing; reduced observed 504s by ~80% in smoke tests.

Next steps / action items:
1) Engineering: implement deterministic cohort timer reset on stream restart (owner: @Ethan Park, follow PR #2891). Target: revert lingering state immediately when a stream is closed/reopened.
2) SRE: roll out tenant config to all NimbleRetail routes and monitor for 24h (owner: @Marta Ruiz).
3) Product/Docs: add guidance for customers about safe stream reuse patterns and recommended client retry jitter (owner: @Aisha Khan).
4) Postmortem: schedule follow-up review with continuous-batcher owners and include workload patterns that trigger the state drift.

Customer impact summary: NimbleRetail observed degraded tail latency impacting chat user sessions intermittently. No data loss. Multiple support alerts and escalations to customer success.

title: Intermittent websocket half-close after corporate TLS rekey -> trailing 'done' envelope never arrives
preview: Issue summary: Customer reports intermittent websocket sessions that appear to close from the server side after a TLS rekey performed by their corporate TLS-inspecting proxy. The stream is truncated: the final JSON envelope with status/done metadata is never received, leaving the client waiting indefinitely. Impact: Multiple production users at Acme AI Labs experience truncated generations and must reissue requests; affects business-critical inference pipelines. Observed first at ~2026-03-09 22:15 PST and recurred several times. Environment: prod cluster behind ALB -> CloudFront -> corporate TLS-inspection -> customer client. No code changes on Redwood side around times of first occurrence.

title: Long-lived keepalive connections from small-stream clients holding GPU slots causing regional capacity squeeze
preview:
Issue summary:
BrightForms is reporting intermittent throughput collapse for their dedicated pool in us-east starting 2026-03-10 11:40 UTC. Symptoms align with a regional GPU supply squeeze where many GPUs appear allocated but traffic shows a high proportion of very short inference streams with persistent TCP/TLS keepalive.

Impact:
- Customer-facing routes (real-time chat) p95/p99 latency sharply increased; observed request queues and autoscaler unable to reclaim GPUs quickly.
- Other tenants in the same reserved cluster experienced degraded throughput and increased fallbacks to lower-cap models.
- Business impact: customer reported degraded UX for production traffic across multiple endpoints.

Timeline & quick facts:
- 2026-03-10 11:38 UTC: BrightForms first observed degraded latency and opened a support channel.
- 2026-03-10 11:45 UTC: Support identified elevated "active_slot_count" with many connections showing <50ms compute but socket persisted >60s.
- 2026-03-10 12:05 UTC: Autoscaler attempted to add GPUs but allocation failed due to shortfall in the region (ephemeral shortage reported by infra).

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern):
1) Client opens long-lived streaming connection with frequent keepalive pings but sends only occasional short inference calls (1-5 tokens).
2) Serving runtime pins a GPU slot for the connection to maintain KV/cache affinity.
3) Connection remains open for minutes; batcher cannot efficiently combine tiny requests into useful batches.
4) When many clients behave similarly, GPU slot utilization becomes fragmented: many slots show low compute duty but remain reserved.

Observed metrics/logs (snippets):
- allocator metrics: "active_slots: 182 / 200", "avg_slot_utilization: 0.12"
- serving logs (sample): "INFO 2026-03-10T11:46:21Z connection_id=xyz keepalive=62s last_compute=0.043s pinned_gpu=gpu-17"
- autoscaler: "scale_attempt=1 -> fail: insufficient_available_gpus(region=us-east)"
- kernel-level: no OOMs; NVLink utilization low; PCIe spikes correlate with background snapshotter in other cluster shards.

Hypothesis:
- Not classic noisy tenant heavy compute: instead small-streams + long connections are causing slot linger (affinity retention) which fragments capacity and prevents autoscaler from recovering effective throughput.
- Combined with a short-term regional provisioning lag and a background snapshotter IO wave (seen in other shards), this amplified the impact.

Immediate mitigations applied:
- Support signaled customer to reduce client keepalive to 15s where possible and to batch short requests client-side (temporary).
- On our side SRE applied a short-lived aggressive reclaim heuristic on the cluster (60s idle compute -> allow slot relinquish). This recovered ~30 GPUs back to the pool but needs careful rollback testing.

Next steps (planned):
- Short window: monitor reclaimed capacity metrics (assigned to SRE, see comments).
- Medium: add a scheduler feature flag to allow opportunistic slot preemption for connections with low compute density after a configurable grace period.
- Long: instrument per-connection compute duty cycle and expose it to console for customers to diagnose keepalive-heavy clients.

Requested from customer:
- Example client connection logs (tcp dumps / client-side timestamps) and sample session IDs to correlate.
- Whether they can apply client-side batching or reduce keepalive interval as a workaround.

title: Layered 429s observed during rolling API token swapback causing throughput collapse
preview:
Issue summary: Customer (Acme Analytics, dedicated) reported sudden and sustained 429s beginning ~2026-03-10T14:20Z that coincided with a scheduled rolling API key rotation. Impact: interactive chat and batch embed pipelines degraded; sustained 30-60% request rejection for 20 minutes and client-side retry storms. Environment: prod-us-west (us-west-2), dedicated pool id dp-88. Customer reports that their orchestrator performs aggressive short retries and they observed token swaps in their logs.

Initial hypothesis: rolling credential refresh led to swapback behavior where some ingress proxies temporarily presented rotated keys while backend accounting still counted against the old token windows. This created token-window misalignment and effective fanout of short-lived keys across pooled connections, exhausting tenant burst buckets and triggering layered 429s.

Why ticket created: customer-facing outage with measurable impact and unclear root cause; require SRE/infra follow-up and mitigation guidance for the customer to restore stability and avoid recurrence.

title: SDK retry storm + small connection pool causing serialized streaming requests and intermittent inference timeouts
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports a recent regression in perceived inference latency and intermittent request timeouts for streaming responses. The customer is on a Dedicated deployment and uses our official Go and Python SDKs behind an internal gateway. Impact: API calls that previously returned streaming tokens within ~120–200ms now see long tails up to 6–10s or outright 504/ETIMEDOUT for some requests.

Observed behavior:
- Increased end-to-end latency for streaming calls from a subset of customer hosts beginning 2026-03-09 02:00 UTC.
- Spikes of 429/502/504 correlated with client-side retries and connection churn.
- Host-level connection pools show active connections ~= configured max_connections_per_host, while requests queue on the client.

Expected behavior:
- Streaming responses begin delivering tokens within expected latency envelope; client retries/backoff should not serialize requests behind a small connection pool.

Environment:
- Customer: FluxHealth (dedicated)
- Region: us-east (customer Dedicated capacity pool)
- SDKs: go-sdk v1.6.3, python-sdk v2.0.1 (custom wrapper used in production)
- Gateway: Envoy sidecar with connection pooling to Redwood API

Steps to reproduce (customer repro):
1) From a customer host with default go-sdk config (max_connections_per_host=8, max_retries=6, backoff_initial_ms=50, backoff_multiplier=2, no jitter) start 100 concurrent streaming requests to redwood-open-7b-v2.
2) Observe client-side queueing and a subset of requests timing out after 10s.
3) Reduce max_connections_per_host to 4 and repeat — serialization worsens and tail latency increases.

Logs / traces (redacted):
- Client logs show sequences: "request queued (pool exhausted) -> retry immediate -> socket closed -> retry -> request queued" repeatedly.
- Edge gateway (Envoy) logs show connection churn to the API and many HTTP/1.1 connection resets.
- Serving runtime traces show some requests arriving in bursts but no host-side CPU/GPU saturation during observed windows.

Initial hypothesis:
- Customer SDK configuration (high retry count with aggressive backoff multiplier but NO jitter + small connection pool) is leading to synchronized retry attempts that serialize behind the small connection pool. This effectively causes head-of-line blocking and perceived inference timeouts even though the backend is healthy. Envoy TCP connection churn exacerbates the problem by reducing available idle connections.

Customer impact and business context:
- FluxHealth runs interactive clinical-assistant features; long tail latency and timeouts are causing user-facing failures and a degraded product experience. They are on a committed Dedicated plan and have SLA expectations for latency and availability.

title: WebSocket multiplexing: unacknowledged mid-stream chunks and missing final token on reconnection
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports intermittent WebSocket streaming sessions that appear to resume after a short gap but the conversation misses the final token(s) and client-side shows last chunk unacknowledged. Behavior started after they enabled multiplexed connections and moved workload onto a dedicated pool.

Impact:
- Several end-user chat sessions lose the final portion of generated messages (often the last sentence) after a 5–30s silent period.
- Client sees socket resume (code 1000) or a short reconnect, but server-side logs show last chunk delivered and not marked acknowledged by the multiplex layer.
- Customer reports impact on UX for long-turn responses and higher risk for partial instructions being delivered.

Environment:
- Customer: ParcelScale (dedicated)
- Region: prod us-east-1 (dedicated pool id dp-87)
- API mode: WebSocket multiplexing enabled, single socket transporting multiple chat sessions
- Model: redwood-7b-v2 pinned at v2026-02-28

Steps to reproduce (as reported by customer):
1. Open a multiplexed WebSocket connection and initiate 6 concurrent chat sessions using long-system prompts (~1.5k tokens) that trigger KV-cache growth and fallback to longer context execution.
2. Trigger a long-generation request on one session (expected ~60–90 tokens).
3. Observe that after ~12–25s of generation the socket shows short silence (no events) and then a reconnect event on the client.
4. After reconnect the session resumes but the final token(s) from the previous generation are missing; server-side trace shows final chunk emitted but not confirmed by multiplexing layer.

Observed behavior:
- Server-side: last chunk logged as "stream_chunk_sent" with offsets; multiplex layer logs a "partial-ack timeout" for that session id.
- Client-side: sees a short disconnect/reconnect and final message ending abruptly.
- No explicit 5xx from runtime; no model crash; CPU/GPU utilization within pool normal.

Expected behavior:
- Multiplex layer should guarantee in-order delivery and durable acknowledgement; on transient reconnection the final token should be replayed or redelivered such that client receives a complete message.

Initial logs attached by customer:
- ws_trace.txt (client side): shows disconnect at 2026-03-09T22:14:05Z and reconnect at 2026-03-09T22:14:09Z with no application-level gap markers.
- session_events.json: server sent chunk id 32 at 22:14:04Z; multiplex "ack" event for session id 7 missing.

Temporary workaround provided by customer:
- Reduce multiplexing concurrency to 2 sessions per socket and disable aggressive keepalive coalescing. This reduces frequency but is not acceptable long-term.

Priority/Customer ask:
- Customer requests root cause and mitigation within 48 hours; restoration of end-to-end guarantees or a recommended configuration to avoid data loss on multiplexed sockets.

title: Cohort signal loss during synchronized canary flip caused unpinned routing to quantized variant
preview: Issue summary: During a synchronized canary roll for redwood-v1-large we observed that a small customer cohort lost explicit model pin state and was routed to the quantized variant (redwood-v1-large-q4). Impact: ~1400 requests over a 12m window returned lower-quality outputs and tokenization deltas; customer observed increased hallucination in 18% of affected responses (reported by customer and confirmed by telemetry). Environment: Dedicated tenant on prod:us-east-1. Steps to reproduce: 1) Start a weighted canary (10%) for redwood-v1-large across us-east-1 with prewarm enabled. 2) Trigger an orchestrator snapshot merge (scheduled merge job). 3) Send steady tenant traffic that relies on explicit version pin headers. Observed behaviour: A cohort of sessions lost model pin during the snapshot merge and were routed to redwood-v1-large-q4 (fallback path) instead of honoring tenant pin. Token counts and tokenizer fingerprints differed, leading to evaluation regression triggers from customer QA set. Expected behaviour: Tenant pin must be preserved across orchestrator snapshot merge; fallback routing should not select a different tokenizer family variant when pin is present. Relevant logs (trimmed): "2026-03-10T18:12:02Z control-plane: commit-snapshot nid=8792 merging epoch 412 -> 413" "2026-03-10T18:12:02Z orchestrator: cohort-signals: cohort=tn-998 lost-pin during merge window (prewarm=true)" "2026-03-10T18:12:03Z edge: route-resolver: selected target=redwood-v1-large-q4 weight=0.0->0.10" "2026-03-10T18:12:08Z serving-runtime: tokenization: tokenizer-hash mismatch from request header - expected 0x3b9f, got 0x8a2c" Additional notes: No 5xx spike, no autoscaler activity; issue correlates with coordinated snapshot/commit and edge prewarm probe timing jitter.
